source: Invest Like the Best — AI, semiconductors, and the robotic frontier (EP.385)
summary: AI application layer startups are extremely difficult to invest in with conviction due to lack of defensibility and the Chase Coleman 1% statistic
summary_extended: Baker argues that investing in AI application layer companies with conviction is extremely difficult today, drawing on Chase Coleman's observation that less than 1% of the current global internet market cap was founded in the two years immediately following the launch of Netscape Navigator in 1994. His reasoning is that AI-first application companies are often thin wrappers around large language models with no clear defensibility, and that the truly transformative application-layer companies may not even be founded for another 5-6 years — just as the most valuable internet companies took years to emerge after the browser's invention. As a result, Baker has concentrated his portfolio in AI infrastructure rather than the application layer, accepting the risk that he may be too conservative and missing some early winners. The investment implication is a deliberate underweight on AI SaaS and application startups in favor of infrastructure plays, with Baker openly acknowledging this positioning may cost him some upside in the near term.
detail: Baker is cautious on AI application layer investments, citing: (1) Chase Coleman's statistic that <1% of current global internet market cap was founded in the two years after Netscape Navigator; (2) AI-first app companies are often thin LLM wrappers with unclear defensibility; (3) the best companies may not be founded for 5-6 years. He has concentrated in infrastructure. Acknowledges he may be too conservative and is missing some winners.
confidence_evidence: Baker explicitly states this is his current portfolio positioning and investment philosophy; acknowledges the risk of being too conservative
quote_fragment: investing there today feels really hard to me
